narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, an LSAT score of 14.50, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 (2nd decile) and Year 3 (1st decile). The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 51% chance that you would fail it on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out most strongly to the model. The biggest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers—far below the typical 5th or 6th decile average—suggesting challenges in maintaining strong academic performance later in your studies. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, right around the dataset average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, showing solid prior college achievement. Similarly, your middle family income bracket helped pull the odds toward success, as it aligns with many students who go on to pass the bar. Compared to typical applicants, your LSAT score of 14.50 is notably lower than the average 36.70, though it wasn't the top factor here, and your full-time enrollment plans match the norm for over 90% of students. While these elements paint a mixed picture, the low Year 3 decile carried the most weight, tipping the scales despite your strengths elsewhere. This doesn't define your potential—many students overcome similar hurdles. To strengthen a future application, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework, retake the LSAT for a higher score, and gather strong letters highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic male navigating these challenges. Consider bridge programs or post-baccalaureate options to demonstrate bar-exam readiness. Reapplying with these improvements could shift the model's view in your favor. Keep pushing forward; your story is still unfolding.
